methodology: STRIDE + PASTA-style business-impact scoring + attack-chain mapping (MITRE ATT&CK-tagged) + compensating-controls assessment + OWASP Top 10 / API Security Top 10 mapping
executive_summary: The Payment Onboarding Service ingests customer KYC applications, screens them against a third-party sanctions API, and persists records to a dedicated data store. Nine threats were identified across all six STRIDE categories; two are rated High risk and form the basis of a single plausible attack chain that could result in unauthorized approval of a sanctioned applicant. One compliance control (outbound PII transfer logging) was found partially compliant.
scope: In scope: API Gateway, Onboarding Service, KYC Data Store, Sanctions Screening integration, and the Jenkins deployment pipeline for onboarding-service. Out of scope: downstream ledger posting.

servicenow.description: Change request for sanctions API integration

actors_components.name: Customer
actors_components.description: Submits KYC application via web form

trust_boundaries.description: Untrusted customer traffic entering the API Gateway
Outbound PII to sanctions screening vendor
Deployment pipeline pushing code into production

threats.title: Session token replay
threats.description: Captured session tokens can be replayed from a different device due to missing device-binding.
threats.impact_rationale: Full KYC PII exposure, cross-tenant reach
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 85% - device binding is a well-understood pattern
additional_controls_required: Add anomaly detection for geographically impossible logins
threats.mitigation: Implement device-bound session tokens with short TTL

threats.title: Parameter tampering on applicant status
threats.description: Client-controlled status parameter could allow an applicant to self-approve.
threats.impact_rationale: Would allow sanctioned applicant approval - regulatory exposure
threats.existing_controls: Server-side validation exists but does not cover all status transitions
additional_controls_required: Enforce status transitions via a server-side state machine
Add integration test for illegal transitions
threats.mitigation: Move status transition logic fully server-side with a strict state machine

threats.title: Unsigned internal gRPC payload
threats.description: Internal gRPC calls between gateway and onboarding service are not signed, allowing tampering by a compromised sidecar.
threats.impact_rationale: Partial PII tampering, single-service blast radius
additional_controls_required: Add message-level signing in addition to transport mTLS
threats.mitigation: Add HMAC signing to internal gRPC payloads

threats.title: Unencrypted document field at rest
threats.description: One document attachment field in the KYC data store is stored without column-level encryption.
threats.impact_rationale: Full document exposure if DB is compromised or misconfigured
threats.existing_controls: Disk-level encryption at rest, DB access is IAM-gated
additional_controls_required: Apply column-level encryption to document fields
threats.mitigation: Encrypt document fields at the application layer before write

threats.title: PII sent to sanctions vendor without field minimization
threats.description: Full applicant record is sent to the third-party sanctions API instead of only the fields needed for screening.
threats.impact_rationale: Partial PII exposure if vendor is breached; vendor risk not fully in our control
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 60% - depends on vendor API supporting field-level requests
threats.mitigation: Minimize fields sent to third-party screening API

threats.title: No immutable audit log of screening decisions
threats.description: Screening pass/fail decisions are not written to an append-only audit log, making it hard to prove a decision was made correctly after the fact.
threats.impact_rationale: Compliance/audit exposure - cannot demonstrate correct screening for regulator
threats.existing_controls: Standard application logs exist but are mutable and not access-controlled
additional_controls_required: Write screening decisions to a WORM/append-only audit store
threats.mitigation: Add append-only audit logging for all screening decisions

threats.title: No rate limit on application submission
threats.description: The application submission endpoint has no per-IP or per-account rate limit, allowing a flood of submissions to exhaust review capacity.
threats.impact_rationale: Availability impact on manual review queue, no direct data exposure
threats.existing_controls: API Gateway has a global rate limit, not per-account
additional_controls_required: Add per-account and per-IP rate limiting
threats.mitigation: Implement per-account rate limiting at the gateway

threats.title: CI/CD deploy credential lacks scoping
threats.description: The Jenkins deploy credential has broad SSH access to the production host rather than a scoped deploy-only account.
threats.impact_rationale: Compromise of this credential yields full production host access
threats.existing_controls: Credential stored in Jenkins credential store, not in plaintext
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 75% - scoped service accounts are standard but require ops buy-in
additional_controls_required: Rotate credential and audit access quarterly
threats.mitigation: Scope the CI/CD deploy credential to deploy-only permissions

threats.title: Debug endpoint left enabled in production build
threats.description: A debug/admin endpoint discovered via repo code search is gated by a header check only, not real authentication.
threats.impact_rationale: Could allow bypass of validation logic if reachable externally
threats.existing_controls: Endpoint is not routed externally by current gateway config
additional_controls_required: Remove debug endpoint from production build
Add build-time lint rule to catch debug routes
threats.mitigation: Strip debug endpoints from production builds via build flag

attack_chains.name: Sanctioned applicant self-approval via session replay + parameter tampering
attack_chains.narrative: An attacker captures a valid customer session token (S-1), replays it from a second device to bypass device-based anomaly checks, then exploits the client-controlled status parameter (E-1) to move their own application directly to an 'approved' state, skipping the sanctions screening step (I-2/R-1) entirely. Because screening decisions are not written to an immutable audit log (R-1), the bypass is difficult to detect after the fact.

steps.description: Attacker captures and replays a customer session token from a second device
Attacker tampers with the client-controlled status parameter to force an 'approved' state
Absence of immutable audit logging allows the bypass to go undetected

attack_chains.overall_risk: High - regulatory and reputational exposure if a sanctioned entity is onboarded undetected

attack_chains.name: CI/CD credential compromise leading to malicious deploy
attack_chains.narrative: An attacker who compromises the Jenkins deploy credential (T-2) - via a phished engineer or a leaked token - gains broad SSH access to the production host and can push a malicious build directly, bypassing code review.

steps.description: Attacker obtains the over-scoped Jenkins deploy credential
Malicious build re-enables the debug endpoint to establish persistence

attack_chains.overall_risk: Medium - requires credential compromise as a precondition, but blast radius is full production access

compliance_gaps.gap_description: Full applicant records are transferred to the third-party sanctions vendor instead of only the minimum fields required for screening, exceeding the data minimization principle in the internal data handling policy.
recommended_additional_controls: Implement field-level minimization before the outbound call
Add a quarterly vendor data-flow review
Update the DPA to explicitly enumerate transferred fields

compliance_gaps.gap_description: Deploy credentials are stored securely but are not scoped to least privilege, partially satisfying the access-control policy.
recommended_additional_controls: Create scoped deploy-only service accounts per pipeline
Enforce quarterly credential rotation and access review

recommendations: Implement device-bound session tokens with short TTL to close S-1 (mitigates S-1).
Move applicant status transitions fully server-side behind a strict state machine (mitigates E-1).
Add append-only, access-controlled audit logging for all screening decisions (mitigates R-1, supports AC-1 detection).
Apply data minimization to the outbound sanctions-screening call and update the vendor DPA accordingly (mitigates I-2, closes compliance gap).
Encrypt document fields at the application layer before persistence (mitigates I-1).
Scope the Jenkins deploy credential to a least-privilege, deploy-only service account with quarterly rotation (mitigates T-2, closes compliance gap).
Remove or properly authenticate debug endpoints before production builds ship (mitigates E-2).
Add per-account and per-IP rate limiting on the application submission endpoint (mitigates D-1).
references: NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1 - Guide for Conducting Risk Assessments
